title.en: Building a Data Pipeline: Read → Transform → Write (Capstone)

description.en: The capstone of the Working with Data track. Real data work is never one magic function — it's a pipeline. You INGEST raw files (CSV, JSON), CLEAN and TRANSFORM them (skip bad rows, convert types, derive new fields), AGGREGATE (group and summarize), and OUTPUT a result (JSON, CSV, a report). Learn the ETL shape, why each stage should be a small composable function, why you validate-and-skip instead of crash, and why you stream large data instead of loading it all at once. Then watch the whole track snap together: files → JSON → CSV → dates → pipeline.

story_variants.en:
## Data Work Is a Pipeline, Not a Spell

When you're new, you imagine real data work as one clever function: feed in a messy file, get a clean answer. It never works that way. Production data flows through a **pipeline** — a sequence of small stages, each doing one job, each handing its output to the next. The classic name for this shape is **ETL**: *Extract, Transform, Load.* We'll use plainer words: **ingest, clean/transform, aggregate, output.**

The whole point is that the data gets reshaped step by step. Raw text enters on the left. A clean, summarized result leaves on the right. Nothing in the middle tries to do everything at once.

## The Four Stages

| Stage | What it does | Typical tools |
|---|---|---|
| **Ingest** | Read raw bytes/text into Python rows or dicts | `open`, `csv.DictReader`, `json.load` |
| **Clean / Transform** | Skip bad rows, convert types, derive new fields | `int()`, `float()`, `try/except`, a `for` loop |
| **Aggregate** | Group and summarize (sum, count, average per key) | a `dict`, `collections.defaultdict`, `Counter` |
| **Output** | Write the result somewhere usable | `json.dump`, `csv.writer`, `print` a report |

Read that table top to bottom and you can see the shape of almost every data script you'll ever write. The names of the files change; the four stages don't.

## Rule 1: Validate and Skip, Don't Crash

Real files are dirty. There will be a row with a blank amount, a price that says `"N/A"`, a date typed by a human at 2am. The beginner instinct is to call `int(value)` and let the program explode on row 4,812. The professional instinct is the opposite: **wrap the risky conversion, and skip the row you can't trust.**

```python
for row in reader:
    try:
        amount = int(row["amount"])
    except (ValueError, KeyError):
        continue          # bad row -> skip, keep going
    if amount <= 0:
        continue          # business rule -> ignore
    # ... only clean rows reach here
```

That `continue` is doing heavy lifting. One malformed cell out of a million shouldn't take down the whole run. You filter the garbage out *early*, so every later stage only ever sees good data.

## Rule 2: Each Stage Is a Small Function

This is where the Functional Python track pays off. Don't write one 200-line `do_the_whole_thing()`. Write `ingest()`, `clean()`, `aggregate()`, `output()` — each tiny, each testable on its own, each **composable**. The pipeline is just those functions wired in order:

```python
rows   = ingest(raw_text)
clean  = transform(rows)
totals = aggregate(clean)
output(totals)
```

If the numbers come out wrong, the bug lives in *one* small stage, not smeared across everything. You debug one gear, not the whole machine — exactly the payoff of composition.

## Rule 3: Stream Large Data, Don't Slurp It

`json.load(open(f))` reads the entire file into memory. Fine for a 2KB config; fatal for a 40GB log. `csv.DictReader` is a **lazy iterator** — it yields one row at a time, so you can process a file far bigger than your RAM. The habit: **iterate, transform, and write as you go**, holding only the running totals — not the whole dataset — in memory.

```python
for row in reader:        # one row at a time
    ...                   # transform + accumulate
# memory holds the summary, never the full file
```

When the dataset fits comfortably in memory, slurping is fine and simpler. The skill is knowing it's a *choice*, and reaching for streaming the moment data gets big.

## The Whole Track, In One Picture

Everything in this track was a piece of this pipeline:

| You learned | It becomes the pipeline's... |
|---|---|
| Reading & writing **files** | the ingest + output ends |
| **JSON** parsing | structured ingest / structured output |
| **CSV** with `DictReader` | tabular, row-by-row ingest |
| **Dates** (`strptime`, `date`) | a transform step — strings into real dates |
| **Pipelines (here)** | the glue that wires all four stages |

## Why This Matters

A data pipeline you can read is a data pipeline you can trust. Small stages mean small bugs. Validate-and-skip means a single bad row never wins. Streaming means the same code that handled a thousand rows handles a billion. Master this shape and you can point it at *any* messy file — sales logs, sensor dumps, exported spreadsheets — and reliably turn raw mess into a clean answer.

> **Key idea:** Real data work is a **pipeline**: ingest raw files → clean/transform (skip bad rows, convert types, derive fields) → aggregate (group & summarize) → output a result. Keep each stage a small composable function, *validate and skip* bad rows instead of crashing, and *stream* large data instead of loading it all at once. Files → JSON → CSV → dates → pipeline: that's the whole track.

nodes.label: The Data Pipeline (ETL)
children: Ingest: read CSV / JSON into rows
Clean / Transform: skip bad rows, convert types, derive fields
Track recap: files -> JSON -> CSV -> dates -> pipeline

left.label: int(row['amount']) on every row: one bad cell at row 4,812 crashes the whole run

right.label: try/except + continue: skip the bad row, keep going, only clean data reaches later stages

description.en: Real files are dirty. Validate-and-skip beats crash-on-first-bad-row every time.

executionSteps.description: Ingest + transform: 'food',10 parses cleanly. Aggregate into the dict.
int('bad') raises ValueError -> except -> continue. The malformed row is skipped, not crashing the run.
Parses fine but -3 <= 0 fails the business rule -> continue. Skipped.
Output stage: the aggregated result. Two bad rows skipped, clean answer produced.

output: Data work is a pipeline: ingest -> transform -> aggregate -> output.
Validate and SKIP bad rows; never let one dirty cell crash the run.
Small composable stages + streaming = code that scales from 1K rows to 1B.
files -> JSON -> CSV -> dates -> pipeline. That's the whole track.

instruction.en: Capstone: build a mini end-to-end data pipeline. You're given CSV text of expense records (columns: category, amount) as a string. SPEC: write summarize_expenses(csv_text) that (1) INGESTS the text with csv.DictReader over an io.StringIO, (2) CLEANS/TRANSFORMS each row — convert amount to int, and SKIP any row whose amount is not a valid integer OR is <= 0, (3) AGGREGATES the surviving rows by summing amount per category into a dict, and (4) returns that dict. The given csv_text contains a malformed amount ('oops') and a zero row that MUST both be skipped. The stub prints summarize_expenses(CSV). Expected output: {'food': 18, 'travel': 50}

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import csv, io

CSV = (
    "category,amount\n"
    "food,10\n"
    "travel,50\n"
    "food,8\n"
    "food,oops\n"
    "travel,0\n"
)

def summarize_expenses(csv_text):
    # SPEC: read -> transform/filter -> aggregate -> return dict
    #   1. reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(csv_text))
    #   2. for each row: try int(row['amount']); skip on ValueError; skip if <= 0
    #   3. sum the amount per row['category'] into a totals dict
    #   4. return totals
    pass  # implement to the spec

print(summarize_expenses(CSV))

en: INGEST: wrap the text in io.StringIO and feed it to csv.DictReader — iterating it yields one dict per row, like {'category': 'food', 'amount': '10'}.
TRANSFORM + FILTER: try `amount = int(row['amount'])` inside try/except ValueError, then `continue`. Also `continue` when amount <= 0 — that skips both the 'oops' row and the 0 row.
AGGREGATE: keep a totals dict; for each good row do totals[cat] = totals.get(cat, 0) + amount so new categories start at 0.
Build totals incrementally as you stream rows — you never need to hold the whole file, only the running sums. Return totals at the end.

practice_challenges.test_code:
import csv, io
assert summarize_expenses(CSV) == {'food': 18, 'travel': 50}
assert summarize_expenses('category,amount
') == {}
assert summarize_expenses('category,amount
a,5
a,5
b,2
') == {'a': 10, 'b': 2}
assert summarize_expenses('category,amount
x,bad
x,-1
x,0
') == {}
assert summarize_expenses('category,amount
x,3
x,bad
x,4
') == {'x': 7}
assert summarize_expenses('category,amount
solo,99
') == {'solo': 99}

q.en: What are the four stages of a typical data pipeline (ETL shape)?

en: Compile, link, run, deploy
Ingest (read), clean/transform, aggregate (summarize), output (write)
Import, export, delete, refresh
Open, sort, print, close

q.en: A CSV has one row with a blank amount. What is the professional way to handle it?

en: Let int() crash so the whole run stops on that row
Wrap the conversion in try/except and `continue` to skip the bad row, keeping the run alive
Replace every amount in the file with zero
Delete the entire file and start over

q.en: Why split a pipeline into small functions like ingest(), transform(), aggregate(), output()?

en: It makes the program run on more CPUs automatically
Each stage is small, testable, and composable — so a bug lives in one stage, not smeared across everything
Python requires exactly four functions per file
Small functions are the only way to read a CSV

q.en: Why prefer streaming (csv.DictReader, row-by-row) over loading a whole file into memory for large data?

en: Streaming changes the data into JSON automatically
It lets you process files far bigger than RAM by holding only the running summary, not the whole dataset
Loading the whole file is impossible in Python
Streaming sorts the rows for you
